title: Treehouse Of Horror Xx

jokes.text: Ah, Halloween: the one night when we can walk the streets undetected.
jokes.setup: Vampires speaking about Halloween night
jokes.punchline: They can walk undetected because everyone else is in costume too
jokes.explanation: Clever observation that Halloween is the perfect cover for actual monsters. Well-integrated character humor with good economy.

jokes.text: Hey, lame-os. Turner Classic Movies called. They want their costumes back.
jokes.setup: Bart insulting vampires' appearance
jokes.punchline: Their costumes look like old movie vampires
jokes.explanation: Standard 'X called, they want their Y back' format joke. Competent but predictable structure.

jokes.text: We lame-os? I have a remedy for our predicament. Into this costumery.
jokes.setup: Vampire responding to insult about their appearance
jokes.punchline: Using flowery language and made-up word 'costumery'
jokes.explanation: Vampire's pretentious vocabulary creates mild humor through character-specific speech patterns.

jokes.text: We brought a bottle of booze. Boooooze! Come in! Come in!
jokes.setup: Vampires arrive at door with alcohol
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate enthusiasm and drawn-out 'Boooooze!'
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer character moment - his priorities are immediately clear. The elongated 'Boooooze!' is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Mmm. Good! Beer good. You good.
jokes.setup: Homer drinking with vampires
jokes.punchline: Reduced to caveman-like speech from drinking
jokes.explanation: Homer's devolution into primitive speech is economical and character-appropriate. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: Lighten up, ladies. It's not cheating when you're wearing a costume! Not the face!
jokes.setup: Homer being attacked by female vampires
jokes.punchline: His absurd logic followed by immediate concern for his face
jokes.explanation: Homer's twisted logic about costume-wearing infidelity is absurd, and the quick shift to vanity adds a nice escalation.

jokes.text: Hooray for Different-Face!
jokes.setup: Celebrating Hubert Wong's victory
jokes.punchline: Nelson's casually racist nickname
jokes.explanation: Relies on casual racism for shock value. Character-appropriate for Nelson but feels cheap and dated.

jokes.text: Or should I congratulate your tutor: Amelia, at the Homework Factory! Your victory is tainted! Asterisk! Asterisk!
jokes.setup: Lisa questioning Hubert's legitimacy
jokes.punchline: Dramatic accusations and literally saying 'Asterisk!'
jokes.explanation: Lisa's academic fury is perfectly characterized, and literally saying 'Asterisk!' is a brilliant absurdist touch.

jokes.text: I barely spent any time on this decision. Asterisk! Asterisk! Asterisk!
jokes.setup: Teacher's dismissive response to Lisa's outrage
jokes.punchline: Lisa continuing to literally say 'Asterisk!' with increasing intensity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the asterisk gag, building on the previous moment effectively.

jokes.text: My horoscope told me I'd see something interesting today, but I thought that meant the horoscope itself!
jokes.setup: Teacher commenting on Lisa in detention being surprising
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about horoscopes being boring
jokes.explanation: Decent meta-joke about horoscopes being uninteresting. Self-referential humor works moderately well.

jokes.text: Exactly! Crisscross! Do we have a deal? Thanks, but I'll pass. If I say 'crisscross' one more time, will that change your mind? Listen, you're a persistent fellow, but I... Crisscross! I'm in.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to convince Lisa to agree to his plan
jokes.punchline: Lisa immediately changes her mind after he says 'crisscross' again
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic that the magic word 'crisscross' can change Lisa's mind works as character comedy for both kids.

jokes.text: Bart, have you established your alibi? Yep. I'm at the tennis match. And people will remember you were there? I'm pretty sure they will. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking if Bart has an alibi
jokes.punchline: Visual implication that Bart is being disruptive at tennis match
jokes.explanation: Visual gag suggested by the 'Mm-hmm' sounds implying Bart is causing disruption at the tennis match.

jokes.text: I've been ding-dong-ditched! Crisscross.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Krabappel discovering someone rang her doorbell and ran
jokes.punchline: Bart's satisfied 'Crisscross' callback
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to the crisscross plan. Economical but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Like Mom and Dad say 'snuggle' when what they really mean is 'let's lock the door and hug.'
jokes.setup: Bart explaining euphemisms to Lisa
jokes.punchline: Innocent misunderstanding of parental euphemism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant child-like misunderstanding of adult euphemism. Bart thinks 'snuggle' means 'lock the door and hug' which is both innocent and funny.

jokes.text: 'Ding-dong-ditch' means you kill her, then you throw that ding-dong into a ditch. Geez, pick up a book.
jokes.setup: Lisa confused about what ding-dong-ditch means
jokes.punchline: Bart's violent misinterpretation followed by intellectual condescension
jokes.explanation: Dark and clever wordplay that takes innocent prank terminology and makes it sinister. 'Geez, pick up a book' adds perfect condescending tone.

jokes.text: Yeah, torture and pulverize her. Then ding-dong-ditch the body and we're even.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining Lisa's part of the deal
jokes.punchline: Casual explanation of 't.p.' as 'torture and pulverize'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the dark euphemism gag, turning 't.p.' into something much more sinister.

jokes.text: Oh, Lisa! Come hold this blade so it doesn't slice through my neck.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Krabappel asking Lisa for help
jokes.punchline: Asking potential murderer to help with dangerous blade
jokes.explanation: Dark irony of asking the person who's supposed to kill you to help with a dangerous blade. Sets up the tension nicely.

jokes.text: Slice her like a bagel! - Ah, got it! Thanks, Lisa. Last thing I want is to be sliced in two-- you know, like a bagel.
jokes.setup: Bart encouraging Lisa to kill Mrs. Krabappel
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Krabappel unknowingly references the exact method suggested
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony as Mrs. Krabappel unknowingly describes exactly what Bart wants Lisa to do. Well-structured escalation.

jokes.text: I've been waiting for you... and trying to figure out how girls pee.
jokes.setup: Bart waiting for Lisa in what's presumably a girls' bathroom
jokes.punchline: His innocent curiosity about female anatomy
jokes.explanation: Very character-appropriate curiosity from Bart. Economic and cringe-funny in its innocence.

jokes.text: Crisscross! Like the plaid on me kilt! You're not wearing a kilt. Uh-oh.
jokes.setup: Willie referencing crisscross pattern
jokes.punchline: Realization he's not wearing what he thinks he's wearing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Willie with visual implication about his state of dress. The callback works naturally.

jokes.text: You're no different from me. We're made from the same dinna. It's pronounced D-N-A, you idiot.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to convince Lisa they're alike
jokes.punchline: Bart mispronounces DNA as 'dinna', Lisa corrects him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Bart's ignorance and Lisa's compulsive need to correct. 'Dinna' is a funny mispronunciation.

jokes.text: We start with Grade A beef, feed that to other cows, then kill them and serve the unholy results on a seven-grain bun.
jokes.setup: Krusty describing new burger
jokes.punchline: Casual description of cows eating cows as 'unholy results'
jokes.explanation: Dark corporate satire about food industry practices. 'Unholy results' is a great euphemism for cannibalistic beef.

jokes.text: Burger Squared! The math checks out!
jokes.setup: Name of the new burger product
jokes.punchline: Krusty's confident assertion about mathematical validity
jokes.explanation: Simple math pun that works with the corporate absurdity. Economical and fits Krusty's huckster personality.

jokes.text: Now, Lisa, you're a vegetarian, but these cows have made a different choice.
jokes.setup: Lisa objecting to cows eating cows
jokes.punchline: Treating cannibalism as a lifestyle choice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic treating bovine cannibalism as personal choice. Perfect deadpan delivery implied.

jokes.text: That's my check-cashing arm, you stupid...
jokes.setup: Kent being attacked by zombie
jokes.punchline: His concern is specifically about his check-cashing arm
jokes.explanation: Good character detail about Kent's priorities even while being attacked. Specific and economical.

jokes.text: A little tip: you might want to wash me before you eat me. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Moe about to be eaten by zombies
jokes.punchline: His practical hygiene advice and polite 'thank you'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe moment - practical, self-deprecating, and polite even while being eaten. Great character integration.

jokes.text: How do I know you're not a muncher? - I'm your father and I need your help! I can't make sense of that muncher talk!
jokes.setup: Bart questioning if Homer is a zombie
jokes.punchline: Homer's obvious father statement dismissed as 'muncher talk'
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic where obvious truth is dismissed as zombie talk. Character-appropriate paranoia.

jokes.text: No! That's the last book in the world! Really? Yes! It's-- oh. Go ahead.
jokes.setup: Homer about to bash Bart with a book, Lisa objects
jokes.punchline: Lisa's intellectual concern overridden when she sees what book it is
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - her intellectualism overrides family loyalty when she sees it's a bad book.

jokes.text: You mean tell her I love her? Or I meaning you? Mm, I'll just high-five her.
jokes.setup: Hibbert's emotional goodbye message
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about pronouns, settling on high-five
jokes.explanation: Homer's grammatical confusion and awkward solution is character-appropriate. The high-five is a good deflation.

jokes.text: Another politician who can't keep his promises.
jokes.setup: Arnold Schwarzenegger robot saying 'Come with me if you want to live'
jokes.punchline: Homer's political commentary
jokes.explanation: Topical political humor referencing Schwarzenegger's governorship. Economical and timely for the era.

jokes.text: Well, as a vegetarian, I did not consume any tainted burgers, and as a convenience store owner, I am armed to the teeth.
jokes.setup: Explaining how Apu survived zombie outbreak
jokes.punchline: Two very different but practical reasons
jokes.explanation: Perfect Apu character integration - his vegetarianism and convenience store experience both relevant. Good escalation.

jokes.text: I can't shoot her. She's Lisa's godmother. You can apologize in hell! I guess I could.
jokes.setup: Homer reluctant to shoot zombie
jokes.punchline: Lisa's violent encouragement and Homer's mild agreement
jokes.explanation: Character reversal where Lisa is more violent than Homer. The casual 'I guess I could' is good understatement.

jokes.text: Who gets to carry the chosen one? Ow! Aw, did the chosen one hurt his chosen little noggin?
jokes.setup: Fighting over who carries Bart
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic baby talk about the 'chosen one'
jokes.explanation: Competent callback to chosen one concept with sarcastic baby talk. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Got your nose. Why you...
jokes.setup: Lisa playing innocent nose-stealing game
jokes.punchline: Bart's violent reaction to children's game
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation from innocent game to violence. Economical and character-appropriate for Bart.

jokes.text: I think the better brains is, are you brains a brains?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to speak while becoming zombie
jokes.punchline: Garbled attempt at 'question' with repeated 'brains'
jokes.explanation: Homer's deteriorating speech pattern as he becomes a zombie. Character-appropriate mental decline.

jokes.text: What kind of civilized people eat the body and blood of their savior?
jokes.setup: Safe zone people wanting to eat Bart
jokes.punchline: Inadvertent reference to Christian communion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant religious satire that connects zombie cannibalism to communion. Sharp, subversive, and economical.

jokes.text: And all we had to do was let Bart take a bath in our food. Hey, Lis, want some soup? Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Resolution showing Bart's bath cured everyone
jokes.punchline: Lisa unknowingly eating soup Bart bathed in
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor with Lisa eating Bart's bath water. Effective but relies on shock value.

jokes.text: Must be another mouse in the pipe. Let's see if this cobra solves it.
jokes.setup: Moe having beer tap problems
jokes.punchline: Using cobra as plumbing solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe absurdity - treating cobra as practical plumbing tool. Great character-specific logic.

jokes.text: Like taking beer from a baby!
jokes.setup: Moe finding unattended beer tap
jokes.punchline: Variation on 'taking candy from a baby' but alcohol-focused
jokes.explanation: Standard wordplay on common phrase. Character-appropriate but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Um, Marge, Homer, uh, just ran out saying he don't love you, and he never did. He what?! Help me! That, uh... that's me. I've been taking ventriloquism lessons. Help me or kill me!
jokes.setup: Moe lying about Homer leaving Marge
jokes.punchline: Homer's desperate pleas explained away as ventriloquism
jokes.explanation: Great escalating lies from Moe with increasingly desperate explanations. Character-appropriate scheming.

jokes.text: That, uh... that's me. I've been taking ventriloquism lessons. Help me or kill me!
jokes.setup: Homer's screams audible during Moe's lies
jokes.punchline: Moe's terrible explanation followed by even more obvious cries for help
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the lie. 'Help me or kill me!' makes the ventriloquism excuse even more absurd.

jokes.text: It tastes like cuddling. It tastes like clean clothes. It tastes like hot steaming cocoa mixed with rainbows...
jokes.setup: Marge describing beer brewed with Homer's essence
jokes.punchline: Increasingly abstract and emotional taste descriptions
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation of taste descriptions becoming emotional rather than literal. Musical context helps.

jokes.text: This is the best musical in light years. Light years measure distance, not time. You know what I meant.
jokes.setup: Homer praising musical using wrong scientific term
jokes.punchline: Lisa's correction and Homer's defensive response
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer/Lisa dynamic with his malapropism and her correction. Character-appropriate but familiar.

jokes.text: The reason I left you is simple. I'm gay?! Yeah, read on. It gets gayer.
jokes.setup: Fake letter revealing Homer is supposedly gay
jokes.punchline: Moe's casual promise of escalation
jokes.explanation: Economical escalation promise. 'It gets gayer' is effective as callback setup but relies on shock value.

jokes.text: I want to nibble on Jamaican jerks and teriyaki thighs
jokes.setup: Fake gay Homer singing about his desires
jokes.punchline: Food puns disguised as sexual innuendo in musical number
jokes.explanation: Clever double entendre with food terms. Musical format allows for elaborate wordplay sequence.

jokes.text: The other day, I ran into an Irishman. Oh, really? No, O'Reilly.
jokes.setup: Moe starting to tell story about Irishman
jokes.punchline: Name pun on 'really' vs 'O'Reilly'
jokes.explanation: Standard pun joke that's been done many times. Economical but unoriginal and feels cheap.

jokes.text: My taste for romance is kinda perverse. I can only make love in the back of a hearse. Plus I gotta be dressed as a Civil War nurse.
jokes.setup: Moe warning Marge about his romantic quirks
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and bizarre requirements
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment with bizarre escalating requirements. Creative rhyming and specific enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: And then when I'm finished, I'll go through your purse. But you could do worse.
jokes.setup: Continuing list of Moe's romantic quirks
jokes.punchline: Admission of theft followed by weak sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Great escalation and perfect Moe character work. The casual admission of theft with 'but you could do worse' is brilliant.

jokes.text: Can you still love a man who's half-beer? I always have.
jokes.setup: Homer's vulnerable question about his condition
jokes.punchline: Marge's loving response implying Homer was already half-beer
jokes.explanation: Perfect romantic/comedic moment that captures their relationship. Marge's response suggests Homer was always half-beer to her. Economical and sweet.

tmdb_overview:
Dial "M" for Murder or Press "#" to Return to Main Menu- Lisa is forced into a Hitchcockian murder scheme by Bart.

Don't Have a Cow, Mankind- Springfield is once again overrun by zombies, thanks to Krusty Burger's latest sandwich.

There's No Business Like Moe Business- In a Sweeney Todd spoof, Moe bleeds Homer dry to create the perfect microbrewed great beer.
